title: Drape-moulded fruit bowl in air-dry clay
subtitle: A wide shallow bowl draped over a hemispheric mould
excerpt: A wide shallow fruit bowl made by draping a rolled clay slab over a hemispheric mould. The slab takes the curve of the mould as it firms, then lifts off cleanly once leather-hard. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature, no kiln required. The finished bowl is around 22 cm diameter and decorative only.
sourceNotes: Drape and hump mould forming has a long tradition in pre-wheel ceramic-making across the Mediterranean and Mesoamerica. V&A and Wikimedia museum open-access photography for the historical-form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For air-dry clay this is the finished structural state.
A convex form (a dome, an upturned bowl, a stack of crumpled newspaper) that a clay slab drapes over. The slab takes the outer curve of the mould as it firms.

content.text: A drape-moulded bowl is one of the oldest methods of making a curved vessel without a wheel. A flat slab is rolled, laid over a curved form, and allowed to firm in place; once leather-hard, it lifts off as a bowl. This version uses a 22 cm hemispheric drape mould (a commercial bisque-fired bowl or any clean upturned mixing bowl). Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over three to four days and the finished bowl holds whole dry fruit (apples, pears, oranges). It is not water-tight; cut fruit and damp fruit should sit on a plate inside the bowl.

attrs.title: About the mould
attrs.body: If using an upturned kitchen bowl as the mould, drape it first in a layer of damp cotton (an old tea towel works). The cotton stops the clay sticking to the glaze and lets the bowl release cleanly once leather-hard.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay air-dry handles the large slab better

items.name: Drape mould (hemispheric bowl form, ~22 cm)
items.substitution: A clean upturned mixing bowl draped with damp cotton

items.name: Two wooden slats (8 mm thick)
items.substitution: Two stacks of three coins each as a guide

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the full 1.2 kg of clay for three or four minutes on the board. The clay should feel uniform throughout with no soft pockets or dry crumbs. A large slab needs an even body or it will crack along the soft seams as it dries.
Rolling the slab

Set the two wooden slats parallel on the board, around 35 cm apart. Place the wedged clay between them and press it flat with the heel of the hand, then roll out with the rolling pin from the centre outward. The slats stop the pin from going thinner than 8 mm and keep the slab even. Roll in one direction, then turn the slab a quarter-turn and roll again. Aim for a finished slab around 35 cm by 35 cm at 8 mm thick. A slab thinner than this slumps too much over the mould; thicker than 10 mm dries unevenly and cracks.
Draping over the mould
1. Set up the mould
Set the
hump mould

upside-down on the board, dome side up. A bisque-fired drape mould needs no preparation. A glazed kitchen bowl needs a damp cotton cover to stop the clay sticking and to let the finished slab release cleanly.
2. Lift and lay the slab

Lift the slab by sliding both hands underneath flat. Lift slowly and evenly; rushing it tears a slab this size. Lay the slab over the centre of the mould and let it settle by its own weight. Press the slab gently down onto the dome with the flat of the hand, working from the centre outward to push any air pockets out toward the rim. Smooth the slab against the mould with a damp sponge so the curve takes evenly.
3. Trim the rim

Trim the overhanging slab around the rim of the mould with the fettling knife, leaving an even oversized edge of 5 mm. Don't trim flush yet: the slab shrinks slightly as it firms and a flush trim leaves a gap. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger.
4. Let it firm
Cover the whole mould plus draped slab loosely with a single layer of plastic and leave for six to eight hours. The slab needs to firm to
leather-hard
before lifting. Too soon and the bowl slumps off the mould; too late and the bowl grips the mould and cracks when prised off.
Lifting and finishing the rim

Once leather-hard, turn the mould right-way-up (so the bowl is now sitting on its rim, dome inside). Gently lift the mould out of the bowl. The clay bowl now sits hollow-side-up on the board. Trim the rim flush to a clean even line with the fettling knife. Round the cut edge with a damp finger or a damp sponge. Add a small foot ring if wanted: roll a thin coil 8 mm thick, score and slip the underside of the bowl in a 14 cm circle, press the coil ring onto it, blend the join inside and out.
Drying
Place the bowl right-way-up on the board and cover loosely with plastic for the first twelve hours. Turn the bowl over after twelve hours so the base dries at the same rate as the rim. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes three to four days at room humidity.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the rim and base with 220-grit sandpaper. Do this outdoors or near an open window and wear a mask: dry clay dust is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics or leave plain, then seal the inside and outside with two thin coats of acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. The seal protects the surface from finger oils and minor splashes but does not make the bowl water-tight.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof and not food-safe. The bowl holds whole dry fruit (apples, pears, oranges, lemons). Cut fruit, damp fruit, or anything that releases juice should sit on a small plate inside the bowl. Keep the bowl indoors and away from damp. Do not wash under running water; wipe clean with a barely damp cloth.
Variations
Paper clay air-dry handles the large slab better; the cellulose fibres bridge joins and reduce slab cracking during drying.
Stamp texture into the slab before draping: press a piece of lace, a coarse linen, or a flat leaf onto the soft slab and roll over with the pin. The texture shows on the outside of the bowl.
Cut a fluted rim: instead of trimming a flush rim, cut the slab in shallow scallops around the mould before lifting. The bowl rim becomes wavy rather than level.

items.symptom: Slab tears as it is laid over the mould
items.cause: Slab too thin, or rolled too dry; or lifted too quickly
items.fix: Aim for 8 mm thickness using slat guides; lift the slab slowly with both hands flat; keep the clay covered during prep so it doesn't dry on the board

items.symptom: Bowl grips the mould and cracks when lifted
items.cause: Mould not covered with damp cotton (glazed bowl), or slab left on the mould past leather-hard
items.fix: Always cover a glazed mould in damp cotton; lift the bowl off the mould as soon as it is firm but still slightly cool to the touch

items.cause: Lifted too soon; clay was still soft and slumped
items.fix: Leave the slab on the mould one to two hours longer next time. To rescue a warped bowl: while still leather-hard, gently press the rim back into a circle against the mould and let it firm in place

items.symptom: Crack runs through the base during drying
items.cause: Base dried faster than the walls (typical when the bowl sits hollow-side-up)
items.fix: Turn the bowl upside-down after twelve hours of drying so both sides air out at the same rate; cover loosely with plastic the first twelve hours
